Variant_1.question_text: Your class has decided to celebrate the end of the school year by visiting a place of interest. You are the class prefect. Your teacher has asked you to write a letter to the parents of your classmates to explain your plans. Write your letter. You must include the following: • when and where you are going and details of your travel arrangements • why your class has chosen this place and what you will do there • how the students in your class will benefit from the trip. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your letter is polite and informative. Start your letter ‘Dear Parents,’ and remember to add an appropriate ending.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Task Fulfilment: 15 marks. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of the purpose of writing a letter to inform and persuade; clear awareness of the situation (explaining plans to parents) and audience (parents of classmates); format entirely appropriate for a letter; all required points (when and where you are going with travel details, why the class chose the place and what you will do there, how students will benefit) developed in detail, fully amplified, and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate and polite. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of the purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate for a letter; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, though there may be slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; at least one required point addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of a specific letter format; none of the required points addressed; given information misunderstood or irrelevant; tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. Language: 15 marks. Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate language with very occasional slips; sentence structures varied for particular effects; verb forms largely correct with appropriate tenses consistently used; vocabulary wide and precise; punctuation accurate and helpful; spelling accurate with very occasional slips; paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate language with occasional errors that are slips or due to ambition; sentence structures show some variation for natural fluency; occasional slips in verb forms or tense, but sequence consistent; vocabulary precise to convey intended shades of meaning; punctuation accurate and generally helpful; spelling nearly always accurate; paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate language with errors from ambition not marring clarity; some variety of sentence structures, but may repeat types leading to monotony; errors in irregular verb forms, but tense sequence sufficient for clear progression; simple vocabulary mainly correct with errors in more ambitious words; punctuation generally accurate with possible errors in direct speech; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate with some errors in ambitious words; paragraphs may show some unity though links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning with patches of clear language; some variety of sentence length and structure not always for a specific purpose; errors in verb forms and tense may cause uncertainty; vocabulary adequate to convey meaning with uncertain idiom; punctuation used but not always helpful with occasional errors; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate with errors in difficult words; paragraphs used but may lack unity or coherence. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning clear but errors frequent and serious enough to hamper precision; as per the mark scheme, this band indicates communication is possible but hampered.

Used in modern dentistry because it is malleable or has aesthetic properties. 12. Used in modern medicine or as a drug to alleviate swelling in arthritis or implanted in human tissue in radiation therapy to treat certain cancers. 13. Variant_1.marking_scheme: General Assessment Objectives for Writing: Communicate appropriately, with a clear awareness of purpose, audience and register (W1). Communicate clearly and develop ideas coherently, at word level, at sentence level and at whole text level (W2). Use accurate spelling, punctuation and grammar (W3). Communicate creatively, using a varied range of vocabulary, sentence structures and linguistic devices (W4). For Section 1: Directed Writing, the 30 marks are allocated as follows: Task Fulfilment (15 marks) and Language (15 marks). Task Fulfilment assesses the ability to write an article which communicates information and persuades clearly, accurately and economically, and to carry out the instructions as detailed, including addressing all required points in detail. Band 1 (15–13 marks): Good understanding of purpose; clear awareness of situation and audience; format entirely appropriate; all required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (12–10 marks): An understanding of purpose; an awareness of situation and audience; format appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (9–7 marks): Some understanding of purpose; some awareness of situation and audience; format generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed (and partially or fully developed); given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register. Band 4 (6–4 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to situation and audience; format may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed (and partially or fully developed); given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven. Band 5 (3–1 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; confusion as to situation and audience; little evidence of a specific format; none of the required points addressed; given information misunderstood or irrelevant; tone may be inappropriate. Award 0 marks if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied out the question or parts of it at random, or the question is not attempted. Language assesses the overall accuracy and fluency: Band 1 (15–14 marks): Highly accurate, apart from very occasional slips; sentence structures varied for particular effects; verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used; vocabulary wide and precise; punctuation accurate and helpful; spelling accurate, apart from very occasional slips; paragraphs have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (13–12 marks): Accurate; occasional errors are either slips or caused by ambition; sentence structures show some variation to create some natural fluency; occasional slips in verb forms or tense formation, but sequence consistent and clear throughout; vocabulary precise enough to convey intended shades of meaning; punctuation accurate and generally helpful; spelling nearly always accurate; paragraphs have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (11–10 marks): Mostly accurate; errors from ambition do not mar clarity of communication; some variety of sentence structures, but tendency to repeat sentence types may produce monotonous effect; errors may occur in irregular verb forms, but control of tense sequence sufficient to sustain clear progression of events or ideas; simple vocabulary mainly correct; errors may occur with more ambitious words; punctuation generally accurate and sentence separation correctly marked, but errors may occur e.g. with direct speech; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; some errors in more ambitious words; paragraphs may show some unity, although links may be absent or inappropriate. Band 4 (9–8 marks): Sufficiently accurate to communicate meaning, with patches of clear, accurate language; some variety of sentence length and structure, not always for particular purpose; errors in verb forms and tense consistency may cause uncertainty in sequence of events or disturb ease of communication; vocabulary usually adequate to convey intended meaning; idiom may be uncertain; punctuation used but not always helpful; occasional sentence separation errors; spelling of simple vocabulary accurate; errors in more difficult words; paragraphs used but may lack unity or coherence. Band 5 (7–6 marks): Overall meaning never in doubt, but errors sufficiently frequent and serious to produce an uneasy effect.

Variant_2.question_text: Recently, you and some other members of your class were chosen to take part in a festival. Your Principal is very pleased that you took part. He has asked you to write an article for your school magazine about what you did at the festival. Write your magazine article. You must include the following: • details of the festival and where it was held • what you and the other students did at the festival • why you feel taking part was a benefit to you and the other students and to your school. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make sure your article is lively and informative. Start your article with a suitable headline.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This question is worth 30 marks, divided into 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and 15 marks for Language. For Task Fulfilment: Band 1 (13–15 marks) means good understanding of the purpose to write an informative and persuasive article, clear awareness of the situation and audience (school magazine readers), entirely appropriate format for an article, all required points (details of the festival and where it was held; what you and other students did; why taking part was a benefit) developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised, given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation, and tone and register entirely appropriate. Band 2 (10–12 marks) means an understanding of the purpose, an awareness of situation and audience, appropriate format, all required points addressed but not always developed in detail, given information organised to support personal opinion, and tone and register appropriate. Band 3 (7–9 marks) means some understanding of purpose, some awareness of situation and audience, format generally appropriate, at least two required points addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may not be logically used to support opinion, and tone usually appropriate with possible slips of register. Band 4 (4–6 marks) means only partial understanding of purpose, some confusion as to situation and audience, format may be inappropriate, at least one required point addressed and partially or fully developed, given information may be used irrelevantly, and tone may be uneven. Band 5 (1–3 marks) means misunderstanding of purpose, confusion as to situation and audience, little evidence of a specific format, none of the required points addressed, given information misunderstood or irrelevant, and tone may be inappropriate. A mark of 0 is given if the response is totally incomprehensible, the candidate has merely copied parts of the question, or the question is not attempted. For Language: Band 1 (14–15 marks) means highly accurate language with very occasional slips, varied sentence structures for effect, verb forms largely correct with consistent tenses, wide and precise vocabulary, accurate and helpful punctuation, accurate spelling with very occasional slips, and paragraphs that have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning. Band 2 (12–13 marks) means accurate language with occasional errors from slips or ambition, some variation in sentence structures for natural fluency, occasional slips in verb forms but sequence consistent, vocabulary precise enough for intended shades of meaning, accurate and generally helpful punctuation, nearly always accurate spelling, and paragraphs that have unity, are usually linked, and show some evidence of planning. Band 3 (10–11 marks) means mostly accurate language where errors from ambition do not mar clarity, some variety in sentence structures but with a tendency to repeat types, errors in irregular verb forms but control of tense sequence sufficient, simple vocabulary mainly correct with errors in more ambitious words, generally accurate punctuation with possible errors in direct speech, accurate spelling of simple words with some errors in ambitious ones, and paragraphs that may show some unity though links may be absent. Band 4 (8–9 marks) means sufficiently accurate language to communicate meaning with patches of clear accuracy, some variety in sentence length and structure not always purposeful, errors in verb forms and tense that may disturb sequence, vocabulary adequate for meaning with uncertain idiom, punctuation used but not always helpful with occasional errors, accurate spelling of simple words with errors in difficult ones, and paragraphs used but lacking unity or coherence. Band 5 (6–7 marks) means overall meaning clear but errors frequent and serious enough to impede communication, limited vocabulary with noticeable errors, simple structures often repeated, and faulty punctuation and spelling.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the points in the passage which describe the stages in the development of railways and the benefits these developments brought, and the advantages of train travel nowadays. USE THE MATERIAL FROM THE WHOLE PASSAGE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Stages in the development of railways and the benefits these developments brought • Carriages pulled by slaves along grooves in limestone (in Greece) The advantages of train travel nowadays • Train stations conveniently situated near town / city centres
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 15 marks for identifying and writing down the correct points from the passage. Award 1 mark for each correct point, as follows: Acceptable answers: 1. Carriages pulled by slaves along grooves in limestone (in Greece). 2. Carriages or they ran on wooden rails or tracks hauled by men or animals (in Europe). 3. Trains (on wooden tracks) were used in mining to transport coal or minerals or raw materials (to final destination). 4. Iron placed on (top of) wooden rails increased their lifespan. 5. (Introduction or invention of) moveable points or joints permitted a (greater) variety or range of journeys. 6. (Introduction or invention of international) standard gauge meant long(er) distances or journeys or meant few(er) changes. 7. (Introduction or invention of) steam engines or steam trains could pull several carriages (behind them). 8. Steam trains or mainline railways meant that transporting goods became or was cheaper (than via other methods). 9. (Introduction or invention of) diesel trains meant low(er) operating or maintenance costs. 10. (Introduction or invention of) electric trains have (relatively) cheap(er) running costs. 11. Electric trains are less noisy or create fewer pollutants. 12. Train stations conveniently situated near town or city centres. 13. No security checks (in train stations). 14. No limit to baggage allowance (on trains). 15. Keeping luggage with passengers or you means passengers or you can keep an eye on it or means it isn’t lost or stolen or keeping luggage with passengers or you saves (the time of) having to wait for it to be unloaded. 16. Can be astonishingly or very fast. 17. Flexibility in seating arrangements makes journey enjoyable or relaxing or means families can sit together. 18. Passenger (well-being enhanced by being) able to walk around or go for coffee or meal. 19. Overnight trains save time or save cost of hotel (bills) or save having to get up early for a flight. 20. Provision of (electrical) power points turns trains into mobile offices or means (business) people can work (while travelling) or use or charge laptops or phones or electronic devices. Not accepted: Points that are not directly from the passage or are altered beyond the original meaning. Additional instructions: Accept own words or direct lifting from the passage. Accept answers in sentences or note form. Points 1 and 12 are already given in the question. If the script is entirely a verbatim lift, award 0 marks. If more than one content point appears under a single bullet, award each content point separately if clearly made. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correctly identified point from the following list, up to a maximum of 15 marks. If a point is made in the wrong section, do not award the mark. If a content point depends on information from another point, withhold the mark unless a clear link is made. Acceptable answers: The stages in the development and rise in popularity of the cinema: 1. Racehorse photographed every thousandth of a second by several cameras; 2. Invention of a camera that could take ten consecutive pictures or frames per second using celluloid film; 3. Kinetoscope, a box-shaped device that showed a strip of film carrying images in sequence; 4. Film projector that allows films to be seen on a screen by many people simultaneously, together at once, or at a time; 5. Recorded events such as acrobats and sporting events could be shown repeatedly at no extra cost to the producers; 6. Building of permanent cinemas or permanent venues; 7. Enough or many films produced to create programmes lasting half an hour or to change programmes weekly when the audience became bored; 8. Silent films accompanied by live music from a piano or groups of musicians; 9. Narrators filled in missing parts of the film or story; 10. Sound-on-disc or recorded sound added to silent film; 11. Sound-on-film or sound recorded onto the film added to silent film. Reasons for the continuing popularity of the cinema today: 12. Cinema has been enhanced by improved technology in sound effects or lighting; 13. Sophisticated, better, modern, evolved, or improved camerawork allows the audience to see more clearly the emotions of the characters or actors, or allows the audience to relate characters' situation to their own lives; 14. Films about issues or themes can be a force for good in the world or can shape the way societies or individuals think; 15. Cinema or film can keep or keeps heritages alive; 16. Different or many genres, categories, or types of film cater for all tastes or ensure or provide entertainment or relaxation for all; 17. Wide or big screens in cinemas facilitate, provide, offer, or allow the showing of spectacular or extravagant films better than television; 18. Computer animations, creations, or graphics enhance or improve the quality of modern films; 19. Celebrity actors or film stars might be or are good role models for their fans; 20. Videos or DVDs can be watched at home or provide family entertainment at home. Not accepted: Examples alone for points like issues (e.g., civil liberties or poverty), heritages (e.g., Bollywood or films about ancient epics), or genres (e.g., war films); '10 pictures at once' or '10 pictures at a time' without full context; 'This box-shaped device... sequence' without link to kinetoscope; 'Films' for events; 'Enough of them' for 'enough films' without context; 'Emotions acted out or portrayed' for actors; 'CGI or computer generated images' for animations; 'Computers alone'; 'Actors alone', 'Celebrity stars alone'.

Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the stages in the development of chocolate and its uses in former times, and the benefits of chocolate and the reasons for its continuing popularity today, as outlined in the passage. USE THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 7 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS Stages in the development of chocolate and its uses in former times • Prepared as a drink 2 000 years ago The benefits of chocolate and the reasons for its continuing popularity today • Has positive effects on health by improving circulation
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Acceptable answers include: 1. Prepared as a drink 2000 years ago. 2. In Mayan civilisation, drinking chocolate played an important part in cultural ceremonies. 3. Incorporated into religion of Aztec civilisation (acceptable as a lift or own words version of the text from lines 13–15, which describes how they associated it with gods and humans). 4. Cocoa beans or chocolate became a form of currency in Aztec civilisation. 5. Reached Spain after the Spanish conquest of the Aztecs or introduced to the Spanish elite. 6. Popularity increased with the addition of sugar. 7. Craze spread to other European countries or other parts of Europe. 8. Dutch, English, French or other European countries created cocoa plantations in Central America (accept 'chocolate' for 'cocoa' but not 'plantations' alone). 9. Industrial Revolution brought steam-powered engines or machines which speeded up the processing, manufacture or production. 10. Invention of a press or pressing machine which made chocolate cheaper to produce or make. 11. Press facilitated manufacture of chocolate in pressed or solid form or bars (do not require reference to press if already mentioned in point 10). 12. Invention of conching produced rich taste and smooth texture (accept 'better quality' for 'rich taste and smooth texture'). 13. Has positive benefits on health by improving circulation. 14. Rich source of antioxidants or contains substances which reduce damage to cells in the body or may work against some types of cancer. 15. Consumption of dark chocolate may reduce cholesterol. Not accepted responses include misinterpretations or inventions not in the passage.

Variant_1.question_text: Now use your notes to write a summary in which you state the stages in the development of chocolate and its uses in former times, and the benefits of chocolate and the reasons for its continuing popularity today, as outlined in the passage. This time, you will be awarded up to 5 marks for using your own words wherever possible and for accurate use of language. Your summary, which must be in continuous writing (not note form), must be no longer than 160 words, including the 10 words given below. Begin your summary as follows: Chocolate was first prepared as a drink 2 000 years ago
Variant_1.marking_scheme: The summary is worth up to 5 marks, divided into two categories: Own Words and Use of English, each out of 5 marks, then added together and divided by two, with any half marks raised to the nearest whole number. For Own Words: Award marks based on the following bands: Band 1 (4–5 marks): Sustained attempt to rephrase using own words throughout. Band 2 (3 marks): Noticeable attempt to use own words in most places. Band 3 (2 marks): Recognisable but limited attempt to use own words. Band 4 (1 mark): Wholesale copying of phrases from the passage, but selective and directed at the question. Band 5 (0 marks): Complete transcript with little originality or link to the question. For Use of English: Award marks based on accuracy and sentence structure: Band 1 (4–5 marks): Accurate use of a variety of sentence structures with few errors. Band 2 (3 marks): Generally accurate with some complex structures. Band 3 (2 marks): Some accuracy but frequent errors. Band 4 (1 mark): Frequent serious errors affecting sense. Band 5 (0 marks): Persistent errors throughout. Serious errors include wrong verb forms, tense errors, sentence structure issues, misuse of prepositions, word meaning errors, agreement errors, and replacing full stops with commas. Indicate irrelevance with IR; if entirely irrelevant, award a maximum of 2 marks for style.

Variant_2.question_text: Identify and write down how fishing has been carried out through the ages, and the reasons for the decline in fish stocks and the consequences of this decline, as outlined in the passage. USE THE MATERIAL FROM PARAGRAPH 2 TO PARAGRAPH 8 INCLUSIVE. At this stage, you need NOT use your own words. To help you get started, the first point in each section of notes is done for you. You will be awarded up to 15 marks for content points. MAIN POINTS How fishing has been carried out through the ages • Killing fish with spears common in ancient times The reasons for the decline in fish stocks and the consequences of this decline • In modern times, people assume that the supply of fish in the world’s rivers and oceans is limitless
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 15 marks. Acceptable answers include: 1. Killing fish with spears was common in ancient times. 2. Fishermen struck the surface of the sea to make or encourage frightened fish to jump into nets. 3. Fly fishing using artificial bait in bright colours to attract fish. 4. Using a hook suspended on or attached to a line. 5. Toxic plants used to induce torpor in river fish to make them easy to catch. 6. Use of gill-nets suspended straight down into the water. 7. Trawling, which involves pulling a net behind the boat to haul in fish as the boat moves. 8. Fish processing vessels or ships that catch, clean, fillet, sort, and freeze fish before landing or docking. 9. Nets made of synthetic fibre because they are cheaper, longer-lasting, and require less maintenance. 10. Fish-farming, which is breeding and raising fish commercially in tanks or enclosures. 11. In modern times, people assume that the supply of fish in the world’s rivers and oceans is limitless. 12. Over-fishing because there were no rules or laws, leading to supplies of certain fish dying out. 13. Some countries did not confine their fishing to their own shores and fished off the shores of other countries. 14. Fishermen do not know the size of available fish stocks. 15. Defined or allocated fishing seasons, where fishermen are forced to fish in designated times with no choice. 16. Introduction of fishing quotas, limiting the number of fish fishermen can take or catch. 17. Authorities or governments can pass laws about fishing nets, such as increasing mesh size to enable or allow smaller fish to escape. 18. Attempts are made to educate fishermen about dwindling resources. 19. Struggle for dwindling resources sometimes results in violence. 20. Pirate fishermen who fish without licences or ignore laws designed to preserve fish stocks. Not accepted responses include 'Fishes' for 'fish', 'drifting' for 'trawling', lifts of specific lines without the full point, and phrases like 'There should be laws about nets'. Excess denies credit if additional incorrect information is added.

Variant_1.question_text: Many new students join your school every year. You must include the following: some of the difficulties that you and other students had when you joined the school; suggestions about what the school can do to help new students; how the students and the school will benefit. Cover all three points above in detail. You should make your report polite and informative. Start your report ‘To the Principal’. Remember to give a name and a date.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
Candidates are expected to write a report that communicates information clearly, accurately and economically, between 200 and 300 words, and include the specified points. Marks are awarded as follows: up to 15 marks for Task Fulfilment and up to 15 marks for Language, for a total of 30 marks.

Task Fulfilment (15 marks):
Band 5 (13–15 marks): Very good understanding of the purpose of writing a report; clear awareness of the specified situation and audience (the Principal); text type entirely appropriate as a report; all required points (difficulties faced by students, suggestions for school help, and benefits to students and school) developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate, polite and informative.
Band 4 (10–12 marks): Good understanding of the purpose; an awareness of the specified situation and audience; text type appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate.
Band 3 (7–9 marks): Some understanding of purpose, may lack some focus; some awareness of the specified situation and audience; text type generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed and both are partially or fully developed; given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register.
Band 2 (4–6 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose; some confusion as to the specified situation and audience; text type may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed and partially or fully developed; given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven.
Band 1 (1–3 marks): Misunderstanding of purpose; little awareness of the specified situation and audience; text type inappropriate; required points not addressed or minimally addressed; given information used irrelevantly; tone inappropriate.

Language (15 marks): Marks are awarded for accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar, and for using a range of appropriate vocabulary with register suitable to the audience and context. The bands are as follows:
Band 5 (13–15 marks): Excellent command of language with a wide range of vocabulary and varied sentence structures; spelling, punctuation and grammar almost entirely accurate; style is fluent and engaging.
Band 4 (10–12 marks): Good command of language with a good range of vocabulary and sentence structures; spelling, punctuation and grammar generally accurate with minor errors; style is clear and effective.
Band 3 (7–9 marks): Reasonable command of language with some range of vocabulary and sentence structures; spelling, punctuation and grammar mostly accurate but with some errors; style is generally clear but may lack variety.
Band 2 (4–6 marks): Limited command of language with basic vocabulary and simple sentence structures; spelling, punctuation and grammar have frequent errors; style is basic and may be unclear.
Band 1 (1–3 marks): Poor command of language with very limited vocabulary and repetitive structures; spelling, punctuation and grammar have many errors affecting meaning; style is unclear and ineffective.

For Task Fulfilment (15 marks): Band 5 (13–15 marks): Very good understanding of purpose, which means clear articulation of experience and expression of what is thought, felt and imagined (W1); clear awareness of the specified situation and audience, which involves sequencing facts, ideas and opinions (W2); text type entirely appropriate; all required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised; given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation; tone and register entirely appropriate, using a range of appropriate vocabulary (W3) and register appropriate to audience and context (W4). Band 4 (10–12 marks): Good understanding of purpose (W1); an awareness of the specified situation and audience (W2); text type appropriate; all required points addressed but not always developed in detail; given information organised to support personal opinion; tone and register appropriate (W3 and W4). Band 3 (7–9 marks): Some understanding of purpose, which may lack some focus (W1); some awareness of the specified situation and audience (W2); text type generally appropriate; at least two required points addressed (and both are partially or fully developed); given information may not be logically used to support opinion; tone usually appropriate, although there may be slips of register (W3 and W4). Band 2 (4–6 marks): Only partial understanding of purpose (W1); some confusion as to the specified situation and audience (W2); text type may be inappropriate; at least one of the required points addressed (partially or fully developed); given information may be used irrelevantly; tone may be uneven (W3 and W4). Band 1 (1–3 marks): Only a limited attempt to respond, with minimal understanding of purpose (W1) and little awareness of situation and audience (W2); text type may be unsuitable; few points addressed; tone and register inconsistent or inappropriate (W3 and W4). For Language (15 marks): Although specific bands are not fully detailed in the mark scheme, candidates are awarded marks for accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar (W5), with marks allocated based on the overall quality of language, including vocabulary range and accuracy, as per the assessment objectives.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct content point, up to a maximum of 12 marks. Acceptable answers: 1. Form of transport. 2. Working animals, such as in the logging industry, hauling wagons, or carrying trees. 3. Used in warfare in ancient times. 4. Used in modern wars or warfare in modern times. 5. Featured in religions or had religious significance or symbolism. 6. Said to bring good luck. 7. Ivory or tusks used to make valuable works of art, artistic objects, decorative objects, religious objects, cutlery handles, or piano keys. 8. Keystone species or have a huge impact for good on the environment or have a good impact on the environment. 9. Form of entertainment or used in circuses or displays. 10. Losing their habitats because of the clearing of land for development projects. 11. Killed by farmers who do not want to lose agricultural or grazing ground to national parks. 12. Poaching of ivory or tusks, or poaching, hunting, or killing of elephants for ivory or tusks. 13. Classified or identified as threatened or endangered species. 14. Ivory trade banned in some countries. 15. Many zoos or circuses refuse to display or keep them, or some zoos are publicly criticised by groups, organisations, or charities for their treatment of elephants. 16. Organisations, groups, or charities raise funds to equip anti-poaching patrols or ensure conservation laws are enforced. 17. Elephant refuges or orphanages established. Not accepted: For point 2, examples alone such as working animals in the logging industry, hauling wagons, or carrying trees without mentioning working animals. For point 3, lift of lines 8–9 male elephants as weaponry. For point 4, examples of terrain or bridges alone. For point 5, examples of reincarnated chiefs or leading processions alone. For point 6, example of elephant’s trunk alone. For point 7, ivory was a much sought after commodity. For point 8, lift or own words version of line 26 elephants dig for animals. Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the information in the passage which describes the changes created by the car and the disadvantages the car has brought. Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each correct content point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 12 marks. Award marks in any order. Acceptable answers for the changes created by the car are: 1. Freedom of mobility. 2. Makes travel or journeys faster, or increased speed of travel or journeys, or travel or journeys take less time than on foot. Acceptable answers include: makes travel faster, increased speed of travel, journeys take less time than on foot. Also accept 'ease' as a synonym for 'speed'. 3. Greater social interaction, or wider circle of friends, or increased communication with family. 4. Greater flexibility in work, or possible to live in the country and work in town, or those living in towns or cities could go further afield to work, or working in distant areas became easy, easier, or possible. 5. Sprawling or larger urban areas, or modern cities resulted. 6. The car changed the appearance of whole countries because of road-building schemes, or new roads, or extending existing roads. 7. Door-to-door trips, or makes door-to-door trips possible or easy. Acceptable answers for the disadvantages the car has brought are: 8. Fuel they burn gives off pollutants which damage the body’s defences against diseases. 9. Damage to the environment, or emits greenhouse gases, or causes global warming, or damages the ozone layer. 10. Over a million or millions of or many people involved in road or traffic or car accidents, or over a million or many or frequent road or traffic or car accidents. Acceptable answers include: people killed or injured in accidents, but not 'increase in road or car or traffic accidents' alone. Do not accept 'several or some accidents or people' for 'many'. 11. Whole communities destroyed or devastated when car factories close. Acceptable answers include: 'affected' as a synonym for 'destroyed'. 12. Makes or might make people less inclined to take exercise, or people don’t take exercise. Acceptable answers include: 'sport' as a synonym for 'exercise'. 13. Driving is often stressful. 14. Car or driving encourages anti-social behaviour. 15. Cars cause congestion or traffic jams in cities.

You may find it helpful to use bullet points when listing the content points. You will be awarded up to 12 marks for content points.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: For this question, award 1 mark for each correct content point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 12 marks. Award points in any order. For the origins and spread of sugar: Acceptable answers: 1. Cultivation probably originated in New Guinea (an island in the South Pacific). 2. It spread or extended to other, nearby, or neighbouring islands in the South Pacific. Not accepted: Moved to India alone. 3. With the migration of Pacific islanders, it moved westwards to India, or Pacific islanders took it to India because of migration. 4. Indian merchants traded sugar cane or it with China. 5. When Persians invaded India, they took sugar back to Persia, or Persians took it from India to Persia. 6. Arab peoples learned or saw how sugar was made, or Arabs broke open the secret of sugar-making. Not accepted: This secret was broken open alone. 7. Arab peoples began sugar production in other lands or in lands they conquered. Allow 'they' for 'Arabs' if identified in an attempt at the previous point. Not accepted: The lift of the phrase 'As Arab expansion continued, they began sugar production' alone. 8. European trade with the East included the importation of sugar or it, or Europeans traded with the East in sugar or it. Allow the lift of the phrase 'subsequent centuries of sugar'. Not accepted: Sugar cane for sugar. 9. It spread into the pharmaceutical world or medicines, or it was considered a valuable medicine, or it was used to mask the bitter or unpleasant taste of some medicine. Not accepted: Sugar cane for sugar, or cure for serious illnesses such as tuberculosis alone. For the reasons for the decline in popularity of sugar: Acceptable answers: 10. The link between sugar and obesity is well established. 11. It causes or may cause illnesses linked to gaining too much weight. Allow the lift of the phrase 'those who consume too much weight'. Not accepted: Causes or may cause illness alone. 12. Refined, white, or purified sugar is empty calories, or refined, white, or purified sugar has no nutritional value. Not accepted: Removal of impurities from raw sugar has no nutritional value. 13. Sugar can become or is addictive. Variant_1.question_text: Identify and write down the advantages of out-of-town shops and shopping, and the advantages of town centre shops and shopping, as outlined in the passage.
Variant_1.marking_scheme:
This question is worth 12 marks. Award 1 mark for each correct point identified from the passage, up to a maximum of 12 marks. 

Part A: Advantages of out-of-town shops and shopping: 
- Acceptable answers: Retail parks provide plenty of parking spaces that are generally free of charge. Award 1 mark for this point. 
- Acceptable answers: Drivers are not faced with as much congestion or traffic on the roads, or there is not as much congestion or traffic on the roads, making the travelling experience pleasant and allowing customers to arrive in a relaxed frame of mind. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: 'Not as much congestion/traffic' alone. 
- Acceptable answers: Many stores allow for a wide range of goods to be purchased in a single trip. Award 1 mark for this point. 
- Acceptable answers: Shopping can be done in comfortable temperatures in a mall or under one roof, so weather is not an issue or problem. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: 'Can be done in comfortable temperatures' alone or 'Can be done under one roof' alone. 
- Acceptable answers: Bigger stores offer a greater variety of goods for sale. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: Lift of lines 14–15 'these big stores in town' as it relates to another point. 
- Acceptable answers: Longer opening hours, such as being open late into the evening and every day. Award 1 mark for this point. 
- Acceptable answers: Sometimes able to offer lower prices or cheaper goods. Award 1 mark for this point. 
- Acceptable answers: Multi-national companies are attracted to retail parks. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: Lift of lines 24–26 'because of cheaper'. 

Part B: Advantages of town centre shops and shopping: 
- Acceptable answers: If a town is of historical interest, the shops will generate income from tourists. Award 1 mark for this point. 
- Acceptable answers: A town might achieve its own individual character through its type of shops or distinctive range of shops, as shops can give a town its character or reflect the features of the town. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: Examples alone or complete lift of lines 30–31 'the features of a university town ... to be found there'. 
- Acceptable answers: Income is derived for a town from occasional or planned events, as people visit towns for these events and spend money in shops. Award 1 mark for this point. 
- Acceptable answers: Town centres are lively, busy, or vibrant, and they promote or give residents a sense of civic pride or pride in their town. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: Line 36 'it’s wonderful community' or 'promote a sense of civic pride' alone. 
- Acceptable answers: Shoppers, people, you, they, or we will often spend more than the price of the items bought, such as going for coffee or meeting friends for lunch, which increases local income. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: 'Increasing local income' alone. 
- Acceptable answers: There is a social dimension, giving people an opportunity to relax or take time out at cafes or restaurants. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: Lift of lines 41–42 'many people opportunity' alone or 'many people relax' alone. 
- Acceptable answers: Small businesses flourish in town centres. Award 1 mark for this point. Not accepted: Examples alone. 
- Acceptable answers: Local people can walk, cycle, or take public transport to do their shopping, making it more accessible or easier for them, or there is no need to own or use a car. Award 1 mark for this point.

Advantages of online shopping:
- Online purchases are delivered directly to your door.
- Goods are often cheaper than those bought in-store. Acceptable answers: Goods are cheaper; Items are the cheapest online; They are often less expensive than in-store options. Not accepted: Just 'Cheap' or lifting lines 8–9 of the passage alone, such as 'online items overhead costs' without context.
- Shoppers make further savings as they do not have to pay to travel; or shoppers do not have to pay for fuel; or shoppers do not have to pay for public transport; or shoppers do not have to pay for the coffee or lunch they might have in the shopping centre. - Shoppers are not at the mercy of the elements or weather; or shoppers do not suffer from adverse weather conditions; or shoppers do not get caught in the rain or burned by the sun. Acceptable answers: Shoppers are protected from bad weather; Shopping avoids exposure to rain or sun. Not accepted: No specific exclusions noted.
- Shopping is much quicker or less time consuming than conventional shopping. Acceptable answers: Online shopping is faster; It takes less time than in-store shopping. Not accepted: Just 'Online shopping is quick' or lifting line 17 alone, such as 'weekly groceries in minutes' without context.
- Time is freed up for people to do more interesting or other things. Acceptable answers: It saves time for other activities; People have more time for interesting pursuits. Not accepted: Just 'People can spend time together' alone; or 'Families can do more interesting things' alone; or any suggestion that only families benefit; or just 'More free time' alone.
- Shoppers know immediately, instantly, or at once if the desired article is unavailable or out of stock; or shoppers can quickly make alternative arrangements if the desired article is not available. Acceptable answers: Shoppers can check stock instantly; Shoppers know right away if an item is unavailable and can find alternatives. Not accepted: Just 'Know quickly'.
- The internet or online shopping is always open or available for business; or the internet is open 24/7; or shopping can be done at any time; or online shopping is not restricted to particular opening hours. Acceptable answers: Online shopping is available around the clock; Shoppers can shop at any hour. Not accepted: Lifting lines 26–27 alone, such as 'If you want free to do so!'.

Advantages of in-store shopping:
